text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST AND MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

INDICATION: 63-year-old female with known Stage IIIB pulmonary adenocarcinoma, EGFR exon 21 L858R-mutant, right upper lobe primary. Prior treatment with osimertinib (first-line, 14 months) complicated by systemic and intracranial progression approximately 1 month prior to this study. Brain metastases (right frontal lobe and right cerebellar hemisphere) treated with stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) using frameless linear accelerator-based technique approximately 10 weeks ago. Subsequently initiated second-line systemic therapy with carboplatin, pemetrexed, and bevacizumab (3 cycles completed). Restaging examination to assess treatment response.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast dated approximately 10 weeks prior (progression study demonstrating 3.2 cm right upper lobe mass and 1.4 cm right paratracheal adenopathy); MRI brain with gadolinium dated approximately 10 weeks prior (demonstrating right frontal 1.1 cm and right cerebellar 0.8 cm enhancing lesions, pre-SRS).

TECHNIQUE:
CT: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed with intravenous iohexol contrast (100 mL, injected at 3 mL/sec). Reconstructions performed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes at 3 mm and 1 mm slice thicknesses.
MRI Brain: 3T MRI with standard brain protocol including sagittal T1, axial T2/FLAIR, axial DWI, axial T2*, and axial pre- and post-gadolinium T1-weighted sequences (gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg IV). Volumetric post-contrast MPRAGE acquired.

---

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The right upper lobe primary mass has decreased in size compared to the prior examination. The lesion now measures approximately 2.4 x 2.1 cm in axial cross-section (longest diameter 2.4 cm), reduced from 3.2 x 2.7 cm on the prior study, representing a meaningful decrease in bulk. The mass retains spiculated margins and abuts the posterior wall of the right upper lobe bronchus without convincing endobronchial extension. Internal density is heterogeneous with areas of residual soft tissue attenuation; no frank cavitation. No satellite nodules in the right upper lobe.

The left lung parenchyma is clear. No new nodules. No consolidation or ground-glass opacity in either lung. Trace bibasilar atelectasis, unchanged from prior. Mild background emphysematous changes bilaterally consistent with the patient's known COPD history, unchanged.

The central airways are patent. No endobronchial lesion identified on multiplanar evaluation.

Pleura:
Bilateral pleural spaces are clear. The right-sided pleural effusion that was present on the prior study has resolved. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hilar Structures:
The right paratracheal lymph node (station 4R) measures 0.9 x 0.7 cm in short axis, decreased from 1.4 cm short axis on the prior study. This node no longer meets standard threshold for pathologic enlargement. No new right-sided paratracheal lymphadenopathy. Subcarinal region (station 7): a residual soft tissue density measures approximately 0.7 cm, stable to slightly decreased. No new mediastinal or hilar adenopathy identified. The aortopulmonary window contains a few small lymph nodes, all subcentimeter and not significantly changed.

Heart and Pericardium:
Cardiac silhouette is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcification is minimal.

Chest Wall and Bony Thorax:
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesion within the imaged thoracic skeleton. No rib destruction. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without focal hepatic lesion. No hepatic mass, cyst, or hypervascular/hypovascular abnormality identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein patent.

Gallbladder and Biliary System:
Gallbladder is present without wall thickening, stones, or pericholecystic fluid. Common bile duct measures 4 mm, within normal limits.

Pancreas:
Unremarkable in signal characteristics and morphology. No ductal dilatation. No mass.

Spleen:
Normal in size and attenuation, approximately 10.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension. No focal splenic lesion.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology. No adrenal mass or nodule identified. No suspicious adrenal enlargement.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Bilateral kidneys enhance symmetrically without focal cortical mass. Small bilateral cortical cysts (largest right kidney 0.8 cm), too small to characterize and stable in appearance. No hydronephrosis. No perinephric stranding.

Bladder and Pelvis:
Urinary bladder demonstrates normal wall thickness and is adequately distended. No pelvic adenopathy meeting size criteria. Uterus and adnexal structures are unremarkable in the postmenopausal context. No free fluid.

Retroperitoneum and Lymph Nodes:
No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. Aorta is normal caliber throughout.

Bones (Abdomen and Pelvis):
No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesions within the visualized lumbar spine, sacrum, or pelvic bones. Mild lumbar spondylosis.

---

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST:

Right Frontal Lobe (previously treated SRS target):
The previously identified right frontal lobe enhancing lesion has markedly decreased in size. The lesion now measures approximately 0.6 cm in greatest dimension, reduced from 1.1 cm prior to SRS treatment. The residual focus demonstrates faint, irregular peripheral enhancement on post-gadolinium sequences without substantial surrounding T2/FLAIR signal abnormality. No increase in associated vasogenic edema. No new restricted diffusion in this region. The appearance is most consistent with a favorable treatment response following SRS, with the possibility of early treatment-related changes (radiation effect) also considered.

Right Cerebellar Hemisphere (previously treated SRS target):
The right cerebellar hemispheric lesion has likewise decreased, now measuring approximately 0.4 cm, compared to 0.8 cm prior to SRS. Enhancement has markedly diminished -- the lesion appears as a small punctate focus of faint enhancement with minimal surrounding T2 signal change. No new perilesional edema. No evidence of local mass effect. The evolving appearance is consistent with a robust response following stereotactic radiosurgery.

Remainder of Brain Parenchyma:
No new enhancing lesions identified on post-gadolinium sequences. Careful evaluation of the cortical and subcortical regions, basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, and posterior fossa reveals no additional foci of pathologic enhancement. No new areas of restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarct. No hemorrhage on susceptibility-weighted sequences.

Ventricles and Sulci:
Ventricular system is normal in caliber and configuration. Sulcal pattern is appropriate for age. No midline shift. No transforaminal or transtentorial herniation.

Leptomeninges:
No leptomeningeal enhancement identified.

Calvarium and Scalp:
No calvarial lesion. No scalp abnormality.

Paranasal Sinuses and Orbits:
Mild mucosal thickening in the bilateral maxillary sinuses, nonspecific. Orbits are unremarkable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. RIGHT UPPER LOBE PRIMARY MASS: Decreased in size from 3.2 cm to 2.4 cm (longest diameter) compared to the prior study obtained at the time of progression from osimertinib, reflecting a meaningful reduction in tumor bulk following three cycles of carboplatin, pemetrexed, and bevacizumab. The lesion remains present with persistent spiculated morphology.

2. MEDIASTINAL AND HILAR LYMPHADENOPATHY: Right paratracheal (4R) lymph node has decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm and no longer meets size threshold for pathologic enlargement. Subcarinal region appears stable to slightly improved. Overall reduction in nodal disease burden.

3. PLEURAL EFFUSION: Previously noted right pleural effusion has completely resolved. Bilateral pleural spaces are now clear.

4. BRAIN METASTASES -- TREATED: Both right frontal and right cerebellar SRS-treated lesions have substantially decreased in size and enhancement intensity, consistent with favorable intracranial response following stereotactic radiosurgery delivered approximately 10 weeks prior. No new intracranial lesions.

5. NO DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE: No new hepatic, adrenal, osseous, or extrathoracic metastatic lesions identified on this study.

Overall, the findings are compatible with a partial response in the chest with resolution of pleural effusion following second-line platinum-based combination chemotherapy, and a favorable treatment response of both intracranial lesions following stereotactic radiosurgery.
